displayTitle: Lap vs. Seam: Read the Shape, Then the Address

beats.narration: Two secondary manufacturing discontinuities — the forging lap and the rolling seam. Their shapes give their origins away.

payload.title: LAP VS. SEAM
payload.sub: Two processing discontinuities that betray their origins by shape.

beats.narration: A lap is surface metal folded over and pressed in without fusing — a smooth, curving line at a shallow angle, near flash lines and corners.

payload.heading: The forging lap: folded, not fused
payload.note: LAP: SMOOTH CURVE · SHALLOW ANGLE · CORNERS

beats.narration: A seam is an ingot surface defect rolled into a tight, straight, longitudinal line — so tight you'll need strong, grazing light to catch it.

payload.heading: The rolling seam: tight and straight
payload.note: SEAM: TIGHT + STRAIGHT · USE GRAZING LIGHT

beats.narration: Side by side: lap, a smooth curve; seam, a straight line. Lap near corners; seam down the bar's full length.
So when geometry talks, listen: the shape tells you the process. Read the shape, then read the address.

payload.sub: The geometry tells you the process.
